title: Regression: Left+Right mouse activation broken after .96 — pie opens for a fraction of a second then instantly closes
description:
After the changes in Bringr-93j.96 (mouse activation enhancements — hold delay, multi-button activation, blocking / non-blocking toggle), the simultaneous left + right mouse button activation has stopped working properly. The wheel does open for a fraction of a second, then instantly closes by itself.

So clicking left + right together (the original activation, US-007 / Bringr-93j.7) effectively no longer summons the pie — it flashes open and immediately disappears.

Expected: simultaneous left + right click should still open and keep the pie open exactly as it did before .96. The .96 changes (hold delay, new activation methods, blocking toggle) shouldn't have broken this existing path.

Likely cause: something in the new hold-delay timing or the blocking / release logic from .96 is mis-classifying the event and dismissing the pie immediately after summoning it.

Highest priority — this regresses the app's primary mouse activation.

Related to the .96 enhancement (Bringr-93j.96), the original L+R click activation (Bringr-93j.7), and the Mouse activation section (Bringr-93j.35).
notes:
Diagnostic findings from user testing (NOT a confirmed root cause — needs deep investigation):

- With the "Suppress normal click while waiting" checkbox ENABLED, the L+R activation works fine — even with hold delay set to 0 ms. The user observes this also feels non-blocking in practice (doesn't seem to interfere with other interactions).
- With that checkbox DISABLED, the regression happens, and it's especially noticeable in apps that have context menus on right-click — e.g. Ghostty terminal, or text fields in Google Chrome.
- User's hypothesis: with "Suppress normal click while waiting" off, the right-click event passes through to the underlying app, triggers its context menu, and that takes focus / dismisses the pie almost immediately. That would explain the "flash open then instantly close" behaviour, and why it's worse on apps with context menus.

UX safeguard to consider (one of these, not both):
- Option A: when L+R click activation (or any activation involving the right mouse button) is enabled, automatically enable "Suppress normal click while waiting" — force it on for those activations, since otherwise the pie can't survive the right-click context menu.
- Option B: leave the checkbox user-controlled, but show an alert / warning when the user disables it for a right-click-based activation, explaining that the pie may not work because the right-click context menu will dismiss it, and recommending the checkbox stays on for those activations.

Deep investigation needed: the right-click context menu is a plausible cause but not confirmed. The agent should verify the actual mechanism (and which apps reproduce it) before settling on either the fix or the UX safeguard.

title: System-wide window-drag stutter while PieSwitcher is running — every app affected, drag-start delay
description:
While PieSwitcher is running, dragging any window has a small but very annoying stutter — there's a fraction-of-a-second delay right at the moment I click and start moving the window. It happens specifically when I start dragging really fast.

This affects every single application system-wide, not just the pie menu — basically the whole computer feels like it momentarily slows down at the start of a window drag.

Strong hint: this is almost certainly caused by something PieSwitcher hooks into globally — most likely the global mouse / accessibility event monitoring (e.g. the event tap used for activation, Bringr-93j.7) — processing each incoming event on a path that blocks or delays the OS for a fraction of a second at drag-start time.

Expected: with PieSwitcher running, window dragging should feel instantaneous everywhere — no stutter at the start of the drag, in any app.

Highest priority — this is a system-wide UX regression that affects every macOS app while PieSwitcher is running.

Related to the global event tap activation (Bringr-93j.7) and the broader performance work (Bringr-93j.53).
notes:
Suspected trigger pinpointed: the stutter is tied to the mouse activation that listens for a simultaneous left + right mouse click (US-007 / Bringr-93j.7). The synchronous detection of that click pair on the global event path likely blocks the OS at drag start. Question for the agent: can that detection be made asynchronous (or moved off the hot event path) so it doesn't stall the system event stream during a drag? Make the activation pipeline stutter-free.

This is super important — the implementing agent is free to debug it using any methods they need: logging, Instruments (time profile / sampling), Accessibility inspection, isolating which hook fires on which event, dtrace / sysdiagnose, or anything else useful. A system-wide stutter at drag start isn't the kind of thing that can be diagnosed from code reading alone — give it room to instrument and measure.

title: Rename the app from Bringr to PieSwitcher (code, files, folders, bundle ID)
description:
Rename the entire application from "Bringr" to "PieSwitcher". This is a complete rename — nothing should still say "Bringr" afterwards.

New name: PieSwitcher
New bundle identifier: com.mekedron.PieSwitcher

Scope — rename everywhere, including:
- The app's display name / product name (Bringr -> PieSwitcher).
- The bundle identifier -> com.mekedron.PieSwitcher.
- All code references to "Bringr" (types, symbols, strings, comments, imports, etc.).
- All file names that contain "Bringr" (e.g. the app entry point and any Bringr*.swift files).
- All folder names (the Bringr/ source folder, BringrTests/, etc.).
- The Xcode project, target and scheme names (Bringr.xcodeproj -> PieSwitcher.xcodeproj; target/scheme Bringr -> PieSwitcher).
- Info.plist / entitlements / build-settings entries that reference the name or bundle ID.
- Supporting files that reference the name or build commands (README.md, CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, run.sh, etc.).

After the rename, the project should build and run under the new name with the new bundle ID, and there should be no remaining "Bringr" references anywhere.

CRITICAL — current blocker: after renaming, opening the project file "PieSwitcher.xcodeproj" in Xcode crashes ("Xcode quit unexpectedly"). The renamed Xcode project is broken — most likely a malformed / inconsistent project.pbxproj produced during the rename (e.g. stale Bringr references, mismatched group/file paths, or a corrupted project structure). Acceptance must include: PieSwitcher.xcodeproj opens in Xcode without crashing, and the project still builds and runs under the new name with bundle ID com.mekedron.PieSwitcher.

title: Launch-time Accessibility permission re-grant modal (dev cert reset)
description:
Bringr is built as an unsigned / ad-hoc-signed dev build, so every rebuild produces a fresh code signature. macOS TCC treats each new signature as a different app and revokes the previously granted Accessibility permission. The practical result: on nearly every launch during development `AXIsProcessTrusted()` returns false, so Bringr cannot enumerate/control windows (US-003/US-004) or install its global event tap (US-007) — i.e. it cannot run normally. This blocks day-to-day dev iteration, hence P0.

Today `AppDelegate.applicationDidFinishLaunching` only fires a bare system prompt (`permissions.requestAccess()`). macOS shows that prompt reliably only once per app identity and it offers no re-check step or guidance, which is poor for the repeated re-grant loop we hit on every rebuild.

Port ClipSlop's launch-time permission alert modal. Reference implementation in ~/Projects/ClipSlop:
- Sources/Views/PermissionAlert/PermissionAlertWindow.swift (floating NSWindow, `moveAside()` slides it left so it does not cover the system dialog)
- Sources/Views/PermissionAlert/PermissionAlertView.swift (per-permission Grant -> Validate flow, numbered manual instructions, "don't show again" toggle, Close)
- Sources/App/AppState.swift: `checkPermissionsAfterUpdate()` / `showPermissionAlert()` / `movePermissionAlertAside()` / `dismissPermissionAlert()`

Bringr is Accessibility-only (no Screen Recording), so build the single-permission version — drop ClipSlop's screen-recording card.

What to build:
1. A dedicated launch-time alert window, separate from the existing Preferences pane (Bringr/PreferencesView.swift), that appears when `!permissions.isTrusted` at launch.
2. Reuse the existing `PermissionsManager` (Bringr/PermissionsManager.swift). Do NOT introduce ClipSlop's static `PermissionService` enum — Bringr's manager already has `requestAccess()`, `openAccessibilitySettings()`, `recheck()`, `status`/`PermissionStatus` copy, and `startMonitoring()` (1s poll + `com.apple.accessibility.api` DistributedNotificationCenter observer).
3. Actions in the modal: a "Grant Accessibility Access" button that fires the system prompt and slides the window aside (ClipSlop's `moveAside()`) so it does not cover the system dialog; an "Open System Settings" button (`openAccessibilitySettings()`); and numbered manual steps (System Settings -> Privacy & Security -> Accessibility -> enable Bringr), reusing the copy already in `PermissionStatus.detail`.
4. Auto-dismiss: because `PermissionsManager` already monitors live, the modal should observe `isTrusted` and close itself the instant access is granted (~1s), with no relaunch. This is the key win for the constant re-grant cycle and improves on ClipSlop's manual Validate button.
5. Wire into `AppDelegate.applicationDidFinishLaunching`: keep the existing XCTest guard, call `startMonitoring()`, and if `!isTrusted` present this modal instead of the bare `requestAccess()` prompt.

Menu-bar app note (LSUIElement = true): the window must use a floating level and `NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps:)` to come to front, like ClipSlop's PermissionAlertWindow. Bringr currently uses SwiftUI `Window` scenes (about/preferences) opened via `openWindow(id:)`; an AppKit `NSWindow` subclass is recommended here for full control over floating level + `moveAside()` + transparent titlebar (see Design notes).

Optional "Don't show again" toggle: Bringr has no persisted settings yet; this would add the first `@AppStorage("suppressPermissionAlert")` bool. Nice-to-have, default OFF — a developer who rebuilds constantly probably wants the reminder, so do not block the core flow on it.
design:
- Reference: ClipSlop ~/Projects/ClipSlop/Sources/Views/PermissionAlert/{PermissionAlertWindow,PermissionAlertView}.swift and Sources/App/AppState.swift (checkPermissionsAfterUpdate, showPermissionAlert, movePermissionAlertAside, dismissPermissionAlert).
- Reuse Bringr's PermissionsManager + PermissionStatus copy; do NOT port ClipSlop's static PermissionService enum.
- Single permission only (Accessibility) — remove ClipSlop's Screen Recording card and its live-check branch.
- Window: prefer an AppKit NSWindow subclass (level = .floating, isReleasedWhenClosed = false, transparent titlebar, center, moveAside()) over a SwiftUI Window scene, for full control of the floating level and the move-aside-on-prompt behavior in a menu-bar-only (LSUIElement) app. Owner/coordinator can live on AppDelegate, mirroring ClipSlop's AppState ownership.
- Prefer auto-dismiss by observing PermissionsManager.isTrusted over ClipSlop's manual Validate button — Bringr already monitors permission state live (startMonitoring), which ClipSlop's static enum did not.
- Extends the already-closed US-002 (Bringr-93j.2, Accessibility permission bootstrap); this is the dev-experience hardening of that bootstrap, not a PRD user story.
acceptance_criteria:
- On launch, when Accessibility is NOT granted (and not suppressed), the permission modal appears; when it IS granted, the modal does not appear.
- The modal offers a "Grant Accessibility Access" action (system prompt) and an "Open System Settings" action, plus numbered manual steps for the Accessibility pane.
- Firing the system prompt slides/moves the modal aside so it does not cover the system dialog (ClipSlop moveAside parity).
- When the user grants access in System Settings, the modal auto-dismisses within ~1s with no relaunch, driven by PermissionsManager's existing monitoring (poll + accessibility-changed notification).
- The bare `permissions.requestAccess()` call at launch in AppDelegate.applicationDidFinishLaunching is replaced by the modal flow; the XCTest guard remains so test runs never pop a window or system prompt.
- Unit test (extend BringrTests/PermissionsManagerTests.swift): the launch-decision logic presents the modal when the injected probe returns false and does not when it returns true, using the existing dependency-injection pattern.
- App builds clean: `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends with `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`.

title: US-001: Stand up lint and test tooling
description:
As a developer, I want SwiftLint and an XCTest target wired into the project so that the quality gates can run from story one.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A unit-test target is added to `Bringr.xcodeproj` and contains at least one passing sample test.
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` runs and passes.
- [ ] SwiftLint is integrated (build plugin or invoked binary) with a committed `.swiftlint.yml`.
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` runs clean against the current source.
- [ ] The three quality-gate commands are documented in the README or CLAUDE.md.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests

title: US-002: Accessibility permission bootstrap
description:
As a user, I want Bringr to detect and request Accessibility/Input-Monitoring permission so that it can enumerate and control windows and observe global input.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] On launch, the app checks `AXIsProcessTrusted()` and reflects the current state.
- [ ] If untrusted, the app prompts the user and links to the relevant System Settings pane.
- [ ] Preferences shows current permission status and a "re-check" action.
- [ ] When permission is missing, activation features degrade gracefully (no crash) and surface a clear message.
- [ ] Permission state changes are picked up without requiring a manual relaunch where the OS allows it.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: Audit & fix settings persistence, then adopt user's current settings as new defaults (with Right Command + middle mouse + haptic-on + Light overrides)
description:
The user has been tuning PieSwitcher over many releases and considers their current configuration "the best combination." They want this exact configuration baked in as the new factory defaults so fresh installs match what they've converged on, with a few explicit overrides (see below).

HOWEVER, before the implementer captures the user's current settings as defaults, there is a precondition that must be addressed: not every setting reliably round-trips across quit-and-relaunch today. The user has noticed they sometimes have to re-set the same options after relaunch — they specifically called out keyboard control settings as misbehaving, but the suspicion is broader than one setting. Adopting the user's current configuration as defaults is pointless if some of those values would be lost on the next launch anyway.

So this task is sequential:

PART 1 — Persistence audit and fix.
Walk through every setting exposed in Preferences. For each one, verify the round trip: change it from its current value, quit the app, relaunch, confirm the new value is intact. Anything that fails to persist is a bug to fix in this task. The audit covers every tab and sub-tab (Activation → Keyboard / Mouse / Dwell, Wheel → Appearance / Behavior, Apps → Pinned / Ignored / Sort, Controls → Keyboard / Mouse, etc.) — no setting is exempt.

PART 2 — Snapshot the user's current settings (after Part 1 is green).
With every setting now verified to persist, the implementer reads the user's current preferences from disk (e.g., `defaults read com.mekedron.PieSwitcher` on the user's machine, captured at implementation time, with the user present so questionable values can be discussed) and codifies each one as the new factory default for fresh installs.

PART 3 — Explicit overrides on top of the snapshot.
These are deliberate deviations from "whatever the user currently has":

(a) DEFAULT KEYBOARD SHORTCUT = RIGHT COMMAND, not Right Option.
Rationale: Option is heavily used as the dead-key modifier for special characters in many European keyboard layouts (e.g., `Option-N` → ñ, `Option-U` → ¨). Binding the pie menu to Right Option risks colliding with normal typing for those users. Right Command is much less commonly used as a system-wide modifier, so it is a safer default that still respects the "use a hand-specific modifier" principle from Bringr-93j.111. This change SUPERSEDES the Right Option default specified in Bringr-93j.111 — Right Command becomes the value that ships out of the box.

(b) DEFAULT MOUSE ACTIVATION BUTTON = MIDDLE MOUSE BUTTON.
Rationale: left and right buttons are too easy to fire by accident during normal app use; the middle button is rarely bound elsewhere on macOS and is a natural fit for a "summon a menu" gesture. Combined with the hold-delay setting, this gives a low-collision default.

(c) HAPTIC FEEDBACK = ON by default.
The user's current value already has this on; the explicit call-out is just to make sure the snapshot in Part 2 doesn't accidentally flip it off.

(d) APP APPEARANCE = LIGHT mode by default.
If PieSwitcher exposes an app-level appearance / theme setting (independent of system appearance), default it to Light. If PieSwitcher follows system appearance with no app-level override, this acceptance item is N/A and should be noted as such.

PART 4 — Cascading updates to related beads.
- Bringr-93j.111 (shortcut picker): the default value shown by the picker and the migration "fresh install" branch must now use Right Command, not Right Option. The picker bead's AC about "fresh install: Shortcut 1 = Right Option" is replaced by "Shortcut 1 = Right Command."
- Bringr-93j.112 (onboarding): the "Current activation shortcut" readout, the suggestion chips, and the "Try it now" instruction must reflect Right Command as the default. The suggestion chip set should still include Right Option and Fn as alternatives (so users on layouts where Right Command is awkward can switch with one click), and additionally include Right Command (now the default) so users who clear the slot can restore it without typing.
- The implementer is responsible for not letting these three beads drift out of sync.

This task is the gatekeeper for the user's "best combination" defaults shipping. It depends on Bringr-93j.111 and Bringr-93j.112 because both define behavior whose defaults this task overrides.
acceptance_criteria:
PART 1 — PERSISTENCE AUDIT & FIX
- Every setting exposed in the Preferences window is exercised end-to-end with the following protocol: change it from its current value → quit PieSwitcher → relaunch → confirm the new value is reflected both in the Preferences UI and in the app's runtime behavior.
- The audit covers every tab and sub-tab currently in Preferences (e.g., Activation → Keyboard / Mouse / Dwell, Wheel → Appearance / Behavior, Apps → Pinned / Ignored / Sort, Controls → Keyboard / Mouse, plus any others). No setting is skipped.
- Every setting that fails the round-trip test is fixed in this task. After the fix, the same protocol succeeds for that setting.
- The "keyboard controls" area called out by the user is explicitly verified as part of the audit and explicitly noted as either "was broken, now fixed" or "was already correct" in the task's close notes.
- A written checklist of every setting audited is included in the task's close notes (or linked from there), so the next person can see exactly what was covered.

PART 2 — SNAPSHOT CURRENT SETTINGS AS DEFAULTS
- After Part 1 is green, the implementer captures the user's current configuration (e.g., via `defaults read com.mekedron.PieSwitcher`) WITH THE USER PRESENT, so values that look unusual can be discussed before being adopted.
- Each captured value becomes the new factory default for fresh installs. A fresh install on a clean macOS user account produces a PieSwitcher whose Preferences show the same values the user is using today, with the override exceptions below.
- Settings that are obviously per-instance state and NOT real defaults (e.g., window-frame positions, "last selection per app" caches, Sparkle internals, NSNavPanel panel sizes, recency lists) are filtered out of the snapshot. The snapshot covers actual user preferences, not in-memory caches or system bookkeeping.
- Fresh-install defaults are independent of the user's machine — the snapshot is codified in the app, not read from the user's machine at runtime.

PART 3 — EXPLICIT OVERRIDES
- Default keyboard shortcut on a fresh install: RIGHT COMMAND (Shortcut 1 = Right Command, Shortcut 2 = Not set).
- Default mouse activation button on a fresh install: MIDDLE MOUSE BUTTON (the existing left/right/forward/back options remain available; the default selection moves to middle).
- Haptic feedback is ON by default on a fresh install (matches the user's current value).
- If PieSwitcher has an app-level appearance/theme setting (independent of system appearance), it defaults to Light. If PieSwitcher follows system appearance with no app-level override, this item is marked N/A in the close notes.

PART 4 — CASCADING UPDATES
- Bringr-93j.111: the bead's "Defaults" section is amended to say Right Command (not Right Option) is the fresh-install default. The implementer either updates that bead's text or adds a close-time note clarifying the supersedure.
- Bringr-93j.112: 
  • The "Current activation shortcut" readout on a fresh install shows Right Command.
  • The suggestion chips on screen 1 include Right Command (the default), Right Option, and Fn at minimum.
  • The selected/active suggestion is visually marked (so the user sees Right Command is the current pick on first launch).
  • The "Try it now" copy refers to whichever shortcut is currently set, not a hardcoded name.
- All three beads (this one, Bringr-93j.111, Bringr-93j.112) describe the same defaults — no contradictions remain between them.

VERIFICATION AFTER ALL PARTS
- Wipe PieSwitcher's preferences (e.g., delete the prefs file or use a clean user account), launch the app, and observe:
  • Keyboard activation is Right Command (held alone) and summons the pie menu.
  • Mouse activation is the middle mouse button.
  • Haptic feedback is ON.
  • Light mode is the active appearance (if applicable).
  • All other Preferences values match the snapshot from Part 2.
- Change a representative sample of settings (at least one per tab), quit, relaunch, and confirm every change persisted. This is the regression check that Part 1's fixes hold up.

UNIT TESTS
- Tests cover the persistence round-trip for at least every setting that was found broken in Part 1.
- Tests cover the new fresh-install defaults: a brand-new preferences store yields Right Command as Shortcut 1, middle mouse as the activation button, haptic ON, Light appearance (if app-level), and all snapshot values as captured.
- Tests cover that loading a preferences file written by a previous version (with the old defaults baked in) does NOT silently rewrite the user's existing values to the new defaults — only fresh installs use the new defaults.

DOCS / DISCOVERABILITY
- The README's "Defaults" or "Getting started" section (if one exists) lists the new default keyboard shortcut, default mouse button, and the rationale for choosing Right Command over Right Option, in one or two sentences.

title: Mouse activation polish (middle-click default): adjustable move threshold, new mouseDown-lock checkbox, hold-delay progress indicator, in-app rationale text
description:
Now that the broader refactor (Bringr-93j.102) is closed: middle button activation with a 150 ms hold delay and the "prevent action" (suppress while waiting) checkbox on works quite well as the new baseline. The remaining work is targeted polish on top of that — not a wholesale rewrite.

Things to add / change:

1) Adjustable movement threshold (in pixels).
- We added a guard that cancels pie opening if the cursor moves at least 1 pixel during the hold (to keep window dragging fast, see Bringr-93j.94). That 1-pixel threshold is too aggressive in practice: with middle click especially, a user can slightly nudge the mouse by a pixel while genuinely trying to hold still, and the pie gets cancelled — which feels unfriendly.
- Make the threshold adjustable (slider / numeric input), so the user can tune how much cursor movement is allowed during the hold-delay before activation is cancelled.
- A sensible default that's higher than 1 px (a few pixels) should work better, especially for middle click. Single global threshold is fine if simpler than per-activation values.

2) New "Lock mouse-button action" checkbox (separate, off by default).
- Distinct from the existing "suppress normal click while waiting" option. This one is stronger: it completely locks the click action — including the mouseDown event — for the configured button(s) while the activation combination is being held, so the button literally does nothing in the underlying app during the hold.
- Why this is useful: most middle-mouse interactions on macOS fire on mouseUp (closing a tab, opening a link in a new tab, etc.), so locking the entire mouseDown/click for middle button works very cleanly with no perceptible unresponsiveness in normal browsing.
- For LEFT and RIGHT mouse buttons, the same lock would cause noticeable unresponsiveness (text-selection drag, link opening, context menu all start on mouseDown), so this checkbox should be off by default and the user warned / educated about that in the settings text.
- Default: OFF.

3) Hold-delay progress indicator.
- When any hold-delay timer is running, draw a progress circle around the cursor that visually fills as the hold-delay counts down.
- Applies to BOTH the mouse hold-delay (this task) and the keyboard shortcut hold-delay (Bringr-93j.58) — same visual treatment in both cases for consistency.
- Goal: the user can see exactly how much longer they need to hold before the pie appears.

4) Generous explanatory description in the settings UI.
- This section of Preferences has subtle behaviour and real trade-offs (Bringr-93j.99, Bringr-93j.100, the move-threshold trade-off, the new mouse-button lock checkbox, etc.). Add a generous descriptive blurb in the settings UI itself explaining:
  - Why middle click is the recommended / default activation on macOS: it's one of the most underused mouse buttons — its only common roles are closing tabs and opening links in a new tab in browsers, and it has no scroll behaviour of its own — which makes it the least disruptive button to capture.
  - The challenges with intercepting mouse buttons globally: some apps fire on mouseDown vs mouseUp, suppressing one without the other can leave half-finished interactions, the global event tap can't be allowed to stutter the OS, the 1-px move threshold trade-off, etc.
  - What each setting does and what its trade-offs are (especially the new "lock mouse-button action" checkbox and the movement threshold).
- The aim is that a curious user opening Preferences understands WHY the defaults are what they are and what changing each option will cost them.

Performance constraint stands: no system-wide stutter or lag from the activation hooks (Bringr-93j.94).

Supersedes Bringr-93j.102 (the broader "rethink everything" refactor — turned out to be unnecessary now that middle + 150 ms hold delay + suppress is working well; remaining work is the targeted items above).

Related to mouse activation (Bringr-93j.96), the keyboard hold-delay (Bringr-93j.58), the drag-stutter / move-threshold motivation (Bringr-93j.94), and the closed broader refactor (Bringr-93j.102).

title: "Raise to front" commit elevates random extra apps (Chrome, Xcode, etc.) on top of the chosen window — simplify: preview = commit, restore only on cancel
description:
Reveal mode is set to "Raise to front". The commit step elevates random extra apps that weren't chosen, ending up in front of the actual choice.

Repro:
1. Currently active app is Ghostty Terminal (the second slice in the pie).
2. Open the pie and hover Telegram.
3. Preview correctly raises Telegram on top of Ghostty.
4. Release the mouse to commit on Telegram.
5. Instead of just leaving Telegram on top, it elevates extra random apps too — e.g. Chrome and Xcode (sometimes Zed) — and they land in front of Telegram, so the final z-order has Chrome / Xcode above Telegram even though I only chose Telegram.

Suspected cause: the app stores window positions before preview and tries to restore those positions on commit. But preview itself stacks things on top of each other, so the restore step ends up re-elevating other apps that happened to be in front before the pie was opened.

Proposed simplification (the actual fix being asked for):
- Stop the position-restore step on commit. The preview already raises what we want; just keep that state, with the chosen app / window on top.
- Make preview and commit do the exact same thing: elevate. That removes the separate "fixation" step.
- This should apply at both levels of the pie:
  - Hovering an app: all of that app's windows are elevated to the front.
  - Hovering a specific window (second level): that window is elevated.
- After this change, preview and commit are literally the same code path — no separate fixation needed.

Also apply the same pattern to "Hide others" mode:
- "Hide others" should follow the same preview = commit unification — its commit code should match what preview already does, with no extra restore step on commit.
- The action there is hiding the other apps' windows (keeping them hidden below / behind the activated one); that hidden state simply becomes the final state on commit.
- Together with Bringr-93j.83 (at the window level, don't hide an app's own sibling windows — just raise the hovered one), this gives "Hide others" a clean, consistent behaviour: hide other apps' windows, keep all of the chosen app's windows visible, with the hovered / chosen window raised on top.

Restoration stays for cancellation only:
- If the user cancels (e.g. releases on an empty area outside the pie), restore everything to its original pre-pie state. The restore code is kept for that purpose, in both reveal modes.

So in both modes: preview == commit (no extra work on commit); restore only on cancel.

High priority. Related to the reveal-strategy (Bringr-93j.13), select-and-commit (Bringr-93j.12), cancel / state restoration (Bringr-93j.15), and the window-level "Hide others" simplification (Bringr-93j.83).

title: Chrome with 1 window shows 4 windows in the pie (repro: "Include hidden windows" on + "Hide others" mode)
description:
Google Chrome has only one window, but the pie menu shows four windows for it.

Reproduction requirements:
- "Include hidden windows" is ON.
- Reveal mode is set to "Hide others".

Steps to reproduce:
1. Open the pie menu and select something — e.g. Calendar (the first app in the list, top-most).
2. Then hover on Chromium / Chrome (the second app, top-right).
3. It shows 4 windows for Chrome instead of 1.

So windows are being duplicated / phantoms, specifically under the combination of "Include hidden windows" + "Hide others" reveal mode, after selecting one app and then hovering another. Only the real window(s) should appear.

Related to the phantom-windows work (Bringr-93j.60), the "Include hidden windows" setting (Bringr-93j.50), and the "Hide others" reveal mode (Bringr-93j.13).

title: Rework the Liquid Glass pie menu — current result looks broken (icons blurred, bad selection border, washed out)
description:
The Liquid Glass redesign from Bringr-93j.62 is done, but the result looks terrible and needs a proper rework. Current state screenshot: docs/screenshots/liquid-glass-current-state.png

Problems visible right now:
- App icons aren't visible — they're blurred out by the glass effect. The icons should stay sharp and clearly legible; there probably need to be separate layers so the icons sit above / clear of the glass blur rather than being blurred by it.
- The border that marks the last active window is added in a way that looks wrong (see the detached wedge / outline sticking out in the screenshot). This indicator needs to be rethought and done differently.
- Overall the whole thing looks washed out and low-contrast — basically everything needs to be reworked properly so it actually looks good and the content (icons, text) is clearly readable on top of the glass.

Please try using the apple-docs MCP server to find the relevant Liquid Glass references while doing this. The Apple doc links and the Liquid Glass skill path are listed on Bringr-93j.62.

This reworks the approach taken in Bringr-93j.62.

title: Redesign the pie menu UI to a macOS Liquid Glass look (drop the blue, improve text contrast)
description:
Redesign the look and feel of the pie menu. The current design leans on a blue colour that I don't like — and the text on it isn't contrast enough, so it's hard to read.

Instead, I want the menu to feel like macOS Liquid Glass — that modern translucent glass aesthetic — with good, readable text contrast.

References (provided for this work):
- Liquid Glass skill: /Users/nikita/.claude/skills/swiftui-liquid-glass
- Applying Liquid Glass to custom views: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/Applying-Liquid-Glass-to-custom-views
- Landmarks: Building an app with Liquid Glass: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/Landmarks-Building-an-app-with-Liquid-Glass
- SwiftUI View.glassEffect(_:in:isEnabled:): https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/View/glassEffect(_:in:isEnabled:)
- SwiftUI GlassEffectContainer: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/GlassEffectContainer
- SwiftUI GlassEffectTransition: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/GlassEffectTransition
- SwiftUI GlassButtonStyle: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/GlassButtonStyle

title: US-012 follow-up: previous active app stays above selected app
description: Selecting a window from the radial menu still leaves the app that was frontmost before summon above the chosen app in the global z-order. Need to make commit restore and activation ordering ensure the selected app/window becomes truly frontmost after restore/unhide races.
notes: New reproduction clue from user: selecting/releasing on a second-level window circle works, but selecting/releasing on the first-level app circle leaves the previously active app above the chosen app. Focus fix should target app-level commit path and ensure choosing an app activates/raises one of its windows, not only restores reveal state.
close_reason: Implemented app-slice commit so releasing on the first-level app circle restores reveal state and activates/focuses the selected app's current front window; added regression coverage and verified lint/tests/build/run.

title: US-012 follow-up: selected window keeps losing focus
description: Committing a selected window from the radial menu still often leaves the originally front sibling window in front/focused after release. Strengthen the commit/focus path so the chosen window reliably wins after restore, activation, AX focus, and raise.
close_reason: Adjusted commit focus to AutoRaise-style AX raise before app activation, strengthened app activation, and verified lint/tests/build/launch

title: US-012 follow-up: committed window does not rise or take focus
description:
Reported against US-012 (Bringr-93j.12, closed): committing a window from the wheel does not bring it forward. The chosen window neither rises to the front nor takes keyboard focus, so the core "select and commit" flow is effectively non-functional.

## Symptom
Clicking/releasing on a window slice runs the commit path, but the target window stays behind the current frontmost app — it does not rise and does not become key/main.

## Commit path
RadialMenuWindow.commitSelection -> RadialNavigator.commit -> WindowControl.commit(_:) (Bringr/WindowControl.swift:117) -> raiseAndFocus(_:) (line 101), which calls:
- system.activate(window.app)  -> NSRunningApplication.activate() (line 268)
- system.raise(window)         -> AXUIElementPerformAction(kAXRaiseAction) (line 282)
- system.focusWindow(window)   -> kAXMain + kAXFocused (line 287)

## Suspected causes to investigate
- Silent no-op on stale/missing AX element: every live primitive does `guard let element = elementCache[window] else { return }` (lines 283, 288). If the hide/restore cycle invalidates or fails to repopulate elementCache for the chosen window, raise/focus do nothing and fail silently.
- Activation ordering/race: commit() runs restore() first, and restore() re-activates the prior frontmost app last (line 188) before raiseAndFocus runs. NSRunningApplication.activate() is async, so the prior app may win the race and stay frontmost.
- App activation semantics: plain NSRunningApplication.activate() (no options) may not reliably bring the app forward on macOS 14+; kAXRaiseAction only reorders within the app's own window layer, so without the app actually activating, the window rises behind the frontmost app.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Committing a window reliably raises it to the front and moves keyboard focus to it (key + main), for apps with one or many windows.
- [ ] Works whether the chosen window was previously normal, minimized, or behind other apps.
- [ ] A regression test covers the commit -> restore -> raise/focus ordering so a silent cache-miss or activation race is caught.
- [ ] Verified by build & run: summon, pick a window of a backgrounded app, confirm it comes to front and is focused.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests
- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-012: Select and commit — focus and remember
description:
As a user, I want clicking/releasing on a window to bring it forward and have Bringr remember my choice so that the next summon starts from where I left off.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Committing a window raises it, activates its app, and moves focus to it.
- [ ] All previously hidden apps/windows are restored to their pre-summon state after commit.
- [ ] The chosen window is persisted as the last selection for that app (best-effort match by app + window title/order across restarts).
- [ ] On the next summon, the remembered window for an app is pre-highlighted when present.
- [ ] Persistence and pre-highlight selection logic are unit-tested.

## Quality Gates

title: US-006: Overlay window and radial layout
description:
As a user, I want the wheel to appear instantly at my cursor with evenly placed slices so that interaction is immediate and predictable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A borderless, transparent, floating, click-through-safe overlay window is pre-warmed at launch and shown/hidden rather than recreated.
- [ ] Given N items, slices are laid out evenly around the ring with computed geometry.
- [ ] Hit-testing maps a cursor position to the correct slice (or "none" in the dead zone/outside).
- [ ] v1 slice content is placeholder: app icon/name for app slices; index number and/or title for window slices (no live preview).
- [ ] The overlay appears on the display under the cursor and is positioned at the cursor.
- [ ] No animations. Slice geometry and hit-testing are unit-tested; appearance is verified by build & run.

## Quality Gates

title: US-004: Window control primitives
description:
As the menu, I want primitives to raise/focus a window, hide and restore other apps, and hide and restore other windows of one app so that reveal strategies and selection can be built on them.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can raise a specific window and move focus to it (target app activated, window made main/front).
- [ ] Can hide all apps except a target app, and restore them to their prior state.
- [ ] Can hide all windows of an app except a target window, and restore them.
- [ ] Captures prior z-order/visibility before mutating so it can be restored exactly.
- [ ] A restore call after any sequence returns windows/apps to their pre-summon visibility and ordering.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`

- [ ] Build and run (`pkill -x Bringr 2>/dev/null; open build/Build/Products/Debug/Bringr.app`) and verify the behavior visually

title: US-005: Nestable, non-singleton menu model
description:
As a developer, I want a tree-based menu model with dynamic node providers so that future menus, deeper nesting, and new action types are additive rather than rewrites.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `MenuNode` tree supports child nodes to arbitrary depth.
- [ ] Node content can be static or supplied by a dynamic provider (e.g. "running apps", "windows of app X").
- [ ] A node carries an action (v1 actions: expand-to-children, focus-window); the action set is open for extension.
- [ ] No global/singleton menu instance — a menu is an instantiable definition resolved by a registry keyed on its trigger.
- [ ] Unit tests build the apps→windows tree from enumeration fixtures and assert structure.

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests

title: US-003: Window enumeration service
description:
As the menu, I want a service that reports which apps currently have on-screen windows and the windows belonging to each so that the wheel reflects live state at summon time.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Returns running apps that own at least one normal, on-screen window.
- [ ] Returns per-app window list with a stable identifier, title, and owning app.
- [ ] Excludes Bringr itself and apps with no normal windows (e.g. agents, menu-bar-only apps).
- [ ] Enumeration is invoked fresh on each summon and completes fast enough not to delay the menu (measure and record the timing).
- [ ] Logic is covered by unit tests using injected fixtures (no live system dependency in tests).

## Quality Gates
- [ ] `xcodebuild -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -configuration Debug -derivedDataPath build build` ends in `** BUILD SUCCEEDED **`
- [ ] `swiftlint lint --strict` reports zero violations
- [ ] `xcodebuild test -project Bringr.xcodeproj -scheme Bringr -destination 'platform=macOS'` passes all tests

title: Radial Window Switcher (Bringr v1)
description:
Bringr's single job in v1 is to switch fast between multiple windows of the same app without touching the keyboard. The user summons a radial (pie) menu at the cursor; it shows the apps that currently have open windows; hovering an app reveals a sub-wheel of that app's windows; hovering a window isolates it on screen for visual confirmation; clicking brings it forward and focuses it. The whole flow stays in the wheel — no Cmd-Tab, Mission Control, or Dock guessing.

This document covers v1 only. URLs, files, folders, custom commands, real window previews, user-created menus, and animations are explicitly future work, but the architecture must not foreclose them.

Source PRD: ./tasks/prd-radial-window-switcher-bringr-v1.md

title: Overhaul README: complete feature list, SEO-ready, screenshot scaffolding, and a noticeable Buy Me a Coffee block
description:
The repo already has a README.md (~200 lines), but it has not kept pace with the features that have shipped or been redesigned over the recent set of beads (new activation flows, the Liquid Glass appearance, Sparkle auto-update, the upcoming shortcut picker, onboarding window, settings persistence pass, etc.). It also has not been written with discoverability in mind — search engines, GitHub's "About" sidebar, and first-time visitors should all immediately understand what PieSwitcher is and why they would install it.

This task delivers a top-to-bottom rewrite (or large overhaul) of README.md with four goals, in priority order:

1. ACCURACY. Every feature actually present in the current build is described. Anything the README claims must be true today. Removed features and obsolete instructions are stripped.

2. DISCOVERABILITY (SEO). The README is structured so that someone Googling "macOS pie menu," "Mac radial menu app," "Cmd-Tab alternative macOS," "macOS window switcher," "Mission Control alternative," and similar phrases finds it. Proper heading hierarchy, descriptive alt text, semantic keywords in natural prose (no keyword stuffing), an anchor-link table of contents, and meta-quality opening paragraph.

3. PRESENTATION. The README looks polished on GitHub.com out of the box: a centered hero with the app icon and tagline, a badge row (latest release, macOS requirement, license, downloads, donate), a clear features section, scannable installation and getting-started instructions, and consistent typography throughout. The implementer has latitude on visual polish (banners, dividers, tables) as long as it renders correctly on GitHub.com in both light and dark theme.

4. SUPPORT THE PROJECT. A noticeable Buy Me a Coffee block ties the project to the maintainer's BMC page (buymeacoffee.com/mekedron). It is prominent enough that someone who likes the app sees it without scrolling looking for it, but it is not so loud that it overshadows the product itself.

SCOPE NOTES:
- Screenshots will be supplied by the user later. The README must include clearly-labeled placeholders for each screenshot the user will eventually drop in, with filename conventions, alt-text templates, and captions. The implementer does NOT generate or fake screenshots.
- The README's release-setup section (certificate guide, secrets bootstrap one-liner) is OWNED by Bringr-zkc, not this task. This task either leaves a clearly-marked anchor where Bringr-zkc will inject its content, or links to a separate docs page; it does not duplicate Bringr-zkc's deliverables.
- The "Defaults" section of the README will need a follow-up edit once Bringr-93j.113 (new defaults) ships, since the default keyboard shortcut, default mouse button, etc., change. This task documents whatever the defaults are AT THE TIME OF WRITING and notes that follow-up is required if .113 has not yet shipped.
acceptance_criteria:
REQUIRED SECTIONS (in order of appearance)
- Hero block at the top: app name, one-line tagline, centered app icon (the existing AppIcon asset). Reads well even before any screenshot is added.
- Badge row directly under the hero: latest GitHub Release, minimum macOS version, license (MIT), download count, and a "Buy Me a Coffee" badge linking to https://buymeacoffee.com/mekedron.
- A 2-4 sentence elevator pitch immediately under the badges that includes, in natural prose, the phrases: "macOS pie menu", "window switcher", and "Cmd-Tab alternative" (without keyword-stuffing — the sentences must read naturally).
- A demo / hero screenshot placeholder with a clear caption and a TODO comment noting filename, recommended dimensions, and alt-text template (e.g., `<!-- TODO: hero screenshot at docs/images/hero.png, ≤1600px wide, alt="PieSwitcher pie menu summoned over a macOS desktop showing app icons in a radial layout" -->`).
- Table of Contents with anchor links to every H2 section.
- "Features" section with a complete list of what the app does today (see "Features-list completeness" below).
- "Screenshots" gallery section with 4–6 placeholders, each captioned, each with filename + alt-text template, laid out so dropping in PNGs at the documented paths fills the gallery without further markup edits.
- "Installation" section: download from Releases (link). If Homebrew is configured later, that subsection has a clearly-marked placeholder so Bringr-zkc / the release infra can plug it in.
- "Getting started" section: launches into the onboarding (Bringr-93j.112), default activation shortcut, where Preferences live, and how to summon the menu.
- "Configuration" section: a short overview of what each Preferences tab controls, with a screenshot placeholder for the Preferences window.
- "Requirements" section: macOS version, hardware notes (e.g., haptic feedback requires a supported trackpad).
- "Support the project" section with the noticeable Buy Me a Coffee block (see "Donation block" below).
- "Contributing" section (one short paragraph; if there is no contribution policy yet, says so explicitly).
- "License" section linking to the MIT LICENSE file.
- "Acknowledgments" section if applicable (Sparkle, any third-party assets).

FEATURES-LIST COMPLETENESS
- The Features section reflects every user-facing capability present in the current build, including but not limited to: radial pie menu for app and window switching; multi-display support; configurable activation (keyboard, mouse buttons, dwell, hold-delay); per-button mouse activation controls; left/right modifier distinction on keyboard shortcuts (once Bringr-93j.111 ships); app exclusion list (once Bringr-93j.109 ships); real window titles on the sub-wheel (once Bringr-93j.110 ships); appearance customization including Liquid Glass; haptic feedback; Sparkle auto-update; menu-bar-only (no Dock icon, no main window).
- For features that are scheduled but not yet shipped at the moment of writing, the implementer either omits them or marks them as "Coming soon" with a link to the relevant bead — but does NOT claim them as shipped.
- The list is grouped (e.g., "Activation", "Appearance", "Behavior") so a reader scans it quickly rather than reading a long bullet stream.

SEO REQUIREMENTS
- H1 is the project name plus a short product descriptor (e.g., "PieSwitcher — macOS pie menu and window switcher").
- The opening paragraph functions as a meta-description: under ~160 characters of natural prose summarising what the app is and who it is for, suitable for GitHub's "About" sidebar and search snippets.
- Heading hierarchy is strictly nested (H1 → H2 → H3, no skipped levels).
- The phrases users actually search for appear in section headings and prose without being unnatural: "macOS pie menu", "window switcher for Mac", "Cmd-Tab alternative", "radial menu", "Mission Control alternative", "macOS app switcher".
- Every image (including placeholders) has descriptive alt text suitable for screen readers and image search.
- All internal anchor links in the TOC resolve correctly when previewed on GitHub.
- All outbound links are HTTPS and point to current, non-redirected URLs.

PRESENTATION / POLISH
- The README renders correctly on github.com in both light and dark theme (no invisible text, no broken markdown).
- The hero, badges, and donation block are centered (using GitHub-supported HTML where Markdown alone cannot achieve centering).
- Feature groupings use either tables or grouped bullet lists (not a single 30-item bullet stream).
- Code blocks have language identifiers (`bash`, `swift`, etc.) so syntax highlighting works.
- No broken links, no broken image references, no leftover placeholders without TODO comments explaining what goes in them.
- Existing project style is respected (the previous README's tone and voice can be carried forward — this is not a "make it different for the sake of being different" task).

DONATION BLOCK
- A dedicated "Support the project" section near the bottom (above License) contains:
  • A short, friendly note from the maintainer (one or two sentences) explaining that the app is independently developed and that donations help keep it going. Placeholder copy is acceptable; the implementer leaves it as obviously-placeholder so the user can swap in their own voice.
  • The official Buy Me a Coffee button (the project's standard image or a shields.io badge) linking to https://buymeacoffee.com/mekedron.
  • The link is also exposed in the badge row at the top of the README, so a reader sees the BMC affordance without scrolling.
- The block is visually noticeable — using a centered layout, an HR above and below, or a callout-style blockquote — but is not animated, blinking, or in any way intrusive.
- The link is verified to resolve to a live BMC page (https://buymeacoffee.com/mekedron) and is included verbatim — no shortened URLs.

SCREENSHOT SCAFFOLDING
- A docs/images/ directory exists (created by this task) with a README.md or .gitkeep inside it explaining what each expected filename should contain, recommended dimensions, and the dark/light theme conventions.
- Every screenshot placeholder in the README references a path under docs/images/ and includes a TODO comment naming the expected file. Example: `<!-- TODO: docs/images/preferences-activation.png — Preferences window, Activation tab, default state, light mode -->`
- The user can drop PNGs at the documented paths and the README renders correctly without any further markdown changes.
- Alt-text templates are descriptive enough that they continue to make sense even before the user uploads the actual images.

COORDINATION WITH OTHER BEADS
- Sections owned by Bringr-zkc (release setup guide, secrets bootstrap one-liner) are not duplicated. The README either contains a clearly-marked anchor or a link to a separate docs page where Bringr-zkc will inject its content.
- A short "Defaults" callout near the Getting Started section documents the current default activation shortcut and mouse button. A note in the source markdown reminds the next editor to refresh this after Bringr-93j.113 lands.

VERIFICATION
- The README is previewed on github.com (after pushing) in both light and dark themes, and the layout, badges, and donation block render as designed.
- All anchor links in the TOC are clicked and confirmed to scroll to the right sections.
- All outbound links are clicked and confirmed to load.
- The BMC link is confirmed to land on a live page at https://buymeacoffee.com/mekedron.
- A markdown linter (e.g., `markdownlint`) reports no errors.
- A spell-check pass is run and obvious typos are fixed.

OUT OF SCOPE
- Producing the actual screenshots — the user supplies these.
- Writing the release-setup guide or secrets bootstrap copy — owned by Bringr-zkc.
- Translating the README into other languages.
- Adding analytics, tracking pixels, or anything that loads external scripts.
close_reason: Rewrote README.md to cover the full v1.0 feature set (middle-button mouse default, right-Command keyboard default, Liquid Glass, dwell, exclusion list, two shortcut slots, hide-on-commit, My Apps, collection scope, trackpad haptics, in-wheel keyboard nav, onboarding, Sparkle); added SEO-oriented hero + badges + TOC, screenshot placeholders with filenames/alt-text/captions, and a noticeable Buy Me a Coffee block linking buymeacoffee.com/mekedron. Leaves the release-pipeline section as a pointer to the Bringr-zkc deliverables (workflow + cert guide + bootstrap script) rather than duplicating them.

title: First-launch onboarding window with shortcut setup, try-it, and external-mouse hint; re-openable from the status bar menu
description:
Today PieSwitcher launches silently. There is no welcome flow, no guided shortcut setup, and no way to discover the keyboard activation other than opening Preferences and reading the picker. A first-time user does not know what the app does, what shortcut to hold, or that mouse-button activation even exists.

This task adds a lightweight onboarding window that opens automatically the very first time the app is launched after install, walks the user through confirming or changing their activation shortcut, lets them try it on the spot (the pie menu actually appears in response), and surfaces a short note about mouse-button activation tailored to whether the user is on a built-in trackpad or an external mouse.

The same onboarding window can be re-opened at any time from the status bar icon's menu (e.g., a "Show Welcome…" / "Show Onboarding" item). Users who skip onboarding on first launch, or who want to revisit it later, can do so without restarting the app or resetting their preferences.

Scope is TWO simple screens, kept lightweight and skippable. The first screen handles activation shortcut setup and a live "try it" confirmation. The second screen surfaces a short, context-aware hint about mouse-button activation. The implementer has latitude on the exact text and visual polish; the acceptance criteria pin down the behavior.

This task depends on Bringr-93j.111 (the new shortcut picker) because the onboarding reuses the picker widget to display and edit the current shortcut.
acceptance_criteria:
FIRST-LAUNCH BEHAVIOR
- On the very first launch of PieSwitcher after install (no prior onboarding-completed flag stored), the onboarding window opens automatically a moment after the status bar icon appears, with focus.
- On every subsequent launch, the onboarding window does NOT open automatically.
- Quitting the app from the onboarding window (or closing the onboarding window without finishing) still counts as "seen for now" — onboarding does not re-trigger on the next launch. The user explicitly re-opens it from the menu if they want it back.

STATUS BAR MENU ENTRY
- The status bar icon's menu has a "Show Welcome…" (or equivalently named) item.
- Clicking it opens the onboarding window from screen 1, regardless of how many times it has been opened before.
- Re-opening does NOT reset the user's stored shortcut or any other Preferences value.

SCREEN 1 — SHORTCUT SETUP + TRY-IT
- Heading reads as a friendly welcome plus one short sentence describing what PieSwitcher does ("Summon a wheel of your open windows from anywhere").
- A "Current activation shortcut" area shows the value of Shortcut 1 from the keyboard picker (Bringr-93j.111). On a fresh install this is Right Option.
- The same shortcut picker widget from Bringr-93j.111 is embedded inline so the user can change Shortcut 1 right here. Edits made here persist immediately and are reflected in Preferences (single source of truth).
- A row of SUGGESTED shortcuts is shown as clickable chips. At minimum: "Right Option", "Fn". Acceptable additions: "Right Command", "Caps Lock" (if technically feasible).
- Clicking a suggestion chip:
  • Writes that shortcut into Shortcut 1.
  • Persists the change immediately, no extra confirm.
  • Updates the "Current activation shortcut" readout to match.
  • Visually marks the selected suggestion (so the user can see which one is currently active if it matches a suggestion).
- A "Try it now" area on the same screen tells the user to hold their configured shortcut to summon the menu.
- When the user actually performs the configured shortcut while the onboarding window is visible, the pie menu opens normally over the current screen — confirming end-to-end that the setup works. The onboarding window does not block or eat the input.
- A "Next" button advances to screen 2.

SCREEN 2 — EXTERNAL MOUSE HINT
- The screen explains that the pie menu can ALSO be summoned from a configurable mouse button, and points the user at Preferences → Activation for details.
- The text is tailored based on best-effort detection of the user's pointing devices at the moment the screen is shown:
  • If a non-Apple-Magic-Mouse external pointing device is detected (e.g., a third-party wired or Bluetooth mouse), the heading or first line acknowledges it: "Looks like you have an external mouse — you can summon the pie menu with a mouse button too."
  • If only the built-in trackpad and/or an Apple Magic Mouse / Magic Trackpad is detected, the text falls back to a generic version: "If you ever connect a mouse with extra buttons, you can summon the pie menu with one of them too." The information is still shown — no screen is skipped silently.
  • If device detection is inconclusive for any reason (permission, API limitation), the generic version is shown without an error.
- A "Done" / "Finish" button closes the onboarding window and marks onboarding as completed (so first-launch auto-open does not retrigger).
- A back-arrow / "Back" affordance returns to screen 1 without losing any state.

PERSISTENCE & STATE
- The "onboarding completed" flag persists across relaunch and across system restart.
- Changing the shortcut from the onboarding window is identical, in effect, to changing it from Preferences — same persistence, same runtime effect (no separate code path that could drift).
- Clearing the "onboarding completed" flag (e.g., a hidden debug command or a Preferences reset) causes onboarding to auto-open on next launch — useful for QA but not required to be user-facing.

WINDOW BEHAVIOR
- The onboarding window is a normal, movable, key window — not a modal overlay, not a sheet, not full-screen.
- Closing the window via the red traffic light, Cmd-W, or the "Done" button all dismiss it cleanly with no leak.
- The window has a sensible minimum size and looks correct in both light and dark mode.
- Opening Preferences from elsewhere while onboarding is open does NOT crash; the two windows can coexist.
- Multiple clicks on "Show Welcome…" do not stack multiple onboarding windows — re-clicking brings the existing one to front.

EDGE CASES
- User changes the shortcut in screen 1, then immediately tries it — the new shortcut works on the first attempt, not after a restart.
- User clicks a suggestion that matches the already-current shortcut — no error, the readout simply stays the same.
- User has Accessibility / Input Monitoring permissions NOT granted yet — the onboarding still opens; the "Try it" area additionally points the user at System Settings → Privacy & Security to grant the missing permission (only if it is actually missing).
- User runs onboarding from the menu, makes a change, closes without clicking "Done" — the shortcut change persists (it was committed on selection), but the onboarding-completed flag is unchanged (i.e., if onboarding was previously completed, it stays completed; if it was the first launch and they closed without finishing, it is still treated as "seen").
- Plugging in or unplugging an external mouse while screen 2 is open does NOT have to live-update the text; the detection snapshot at the moment screen 2 opens is sufficient.

UNIT TESTS
- Tests cover the first-launch detection (no flag → open; flag set → do not open).
- Tests cover the "onboarding completed" persistence (write → read back across restarts).
- Tests cover the suggestion-click flow: clicking "Right Option" writes Right Option to Shortcut 1 and persists.
- Tests cover the readout staying in sync with the picker when the user types a shortcut manually.
- Tests cover the external-mouse detection branching (mocked device list → correct text variant chosen).
- Tests cover that the inline picker writes to the same storage as the Preferences picker (single source of truth).
- Tests cover the status-bar menu item opening the onboarding window when invoked, and bringing it to front when it is already open (no duplicates).

DOCS / DISCOVERABILITY
- The README mentions the onboarding flow and the "Show Welcome…" menu item, so support requests like "I can't find where to set the shortcut" have an obvious answer.

title: Replace modifier-checkbox activation with a proper keyboard-shortcut picker (left/right modifier distinction, up to 2 shortcuts)
description:
Today, PieSwitcher's keyboard activation is configured as a set of checkboxes: the user ticks which modifier keys (Option, Command, Shift, Control) trigger the pie menu, and the app fires when ANY of those modifiers is held. This is limiting in two important ways:

1. There is no way to distinguish LEFT Option from RIGHT Option, LEFT Command from RIGHT Command, etc. A user who wants the pie menu on Right Option but not Left Option cannot get that today. This matters because many users keep their left-hand modifiers free for the system (Cmd-C, Cmd-Tab, Option-click in apps) and want the pie menu bound to a hand-specific modifier that does not collide.

2. The current UI cannot express "modifier + key" combinations (e.g., Right Option + Space). Only bare modifiers can be configured.

The user wants a proper keyboard-shortcut picker, modeled on VoiceInk's settings UI (~/Projects/VoiceInk, KeyboardShortcutView + HotkeyManager + MiniRecorderShortcutManager). Each shortcut slot lets the user record either a bare modifier (held alone) or a modifier-plus-key combination, with explicit left/right distinction for Option and Command (and ideally Shift / Control for consistency). The user can configure UP TO TWO shortcuts that both trigger the same activation, so they can have, say, "Right Option (alone)" as the primary and "Caps Lock + Space" as a secondary.

The default shortcut for new installs and for users upgrading from the checkbox-based UI is RIGHT OPTION held alone. This is conservative: Right Option is rarely used by macOS or apps, so the pie menu does not collide with normal typing.

This task replaces the checkbox UI with the picker UI, migrates existing settings as cleanly as possible, and updates the activation engine to honor the left/right distinction at runtime (not just store it visually).
acceptance_criteria:
PREFERENCES UI — PICKER SHAPE
- The modifier-checkboxes UI for keyboard activation is removed from Preferences and replaced with a shortcut-picker block.
- The picker block shows TWO slots labeled "Shortcut 1" and "Shortcut 2" (matching VoiceInk's pattern).
- "Shortcut 1" is always visible. "Shortcut 2" is shown when set; otherwise an "Add second shortcut" button appears underneath "Shortcut 1".
- Once "Shortcut 2" exists, a remove control (e.g., minus button on the row) clears it and brings back the "Add second shortcut" button.
- Each slot displays its current value as rendered key caps (e.g., ⌥, ⌘, ⇧, ⌃) plus any non-modifier key, in the order modifier(s)-then-key. An unset slot shows "Not set" in secondary text.
- The picker's render visually disambiguates LEFT vs RIGHT for Option, Command, Shift, and Control. Acceptable presentations: small "L" / "R" badge on the key cap, distinct cap variants, or a label like "Right Option" next to the icon — whatever the implementer prefers as long as the user can tell at a glance which side is set.

RECORDING A SHORTCUT
- Clicking a slot puts it into capture mode: a brief visual cue (highlight, "Press a shortcut…" placeholder) tells the user the picker is listening.
- The user can record any of the following and the slot accepts it:
  • A bare modifier held alone (e.g., Right Option, Left Command, Right Shift). The picker captures the modifier when held for a short threshold (no other key pressed) and commits when released.
  • A modifier + non-modifier key combination (e.g., Right Option + Space, Left Command + `).
  • Combinations of multiple modifiers + a non-modifier key (e.g., Right Option + Right Shift + K).
- The left/right distinction is preserved from the moment of capture through to display, persistence, and runtime matching. Capturing Right Option does NOT also capture Left Option.
- While in capture mode, Escape cancels the capture and leaves the slot at its previous value.
- While in capture mode, the slot does not commit until either a valid shortcut is held or the user presses Escape — accidental partial keypresses do not corrupt the saved value.
- A "Clear" / "Reset to default" control on the slot returns it to its default value (for Shortcut 1: Right Option; for Shortcut 2: unset).

RUNTIME MATCHING
- Holding Right Option fires the activation when (and only when) the configured shortcut is Right Option. Left Option does NOT fire it.
- The same rule applies to Right vs Left Command, Right vs Left Shift, Right vs Left Control.
- For modifier+key shortcuts, the activation fires only when the exact configured combination is pressed; partial matches (only the modifier, or only the key) do not.
- Either of the two configured shortcuts independently triggers the activation. The user does not have to choose between Shortcut 1 and Shortcut 2 — both work simultaneously.
- An unset Shortcut 2 has no effect: the activation does not fire by accident.
- If both slots are cleared (both "Not set"), keyboard activation is disabled — the user can still summon the menu via mouse if a mouse-button activation is configured.

DEFAULTS
- For a fresh install: Shortcut 1 = Right Option (held alone). Shortcut 2 = Not set.
- The default is applied even if the user has never opened the Preferences window.

MIGRATION FROM THE CHECKBOX UI
- Users who had a single modifier ticked in the old UI keep that modifier as Shortcut 1 after upgrading, with NO left/right preference (i.e., it matches either side — see "back-compat" below). The new default of "Right Option only" applies only to fresh installs, not to upgrades.
- Users who had multiple modifiers ticked map to: Shortcut 1 = the first ticked modifier (in a deterministic order), Shortcut 2 = the second ticked modifier if any, with no-left/right-preference matching for both. Any modifiers beyond the second are dropped, and a one-time in-app notice tells the user their configuration was migrated and points to Preferences.
- BACK-COMPAT MATCHING: migrated shortcuts that came from the old checkbox UI fire on EITHER side of the modifier (Left OR Right) so behavior is preserved for the upgrading user. Newly-recorded shortcuts (entered after this feature ships) are side-specific based on what the user pressed.
- No user loses their activation configuration silently. If migration is ambiguous (e.g., zero modifiers ticked), the new default (Right Option) is applied AND a one-time notice surfaces.

PERSISTENCE
- Both shortcut slots survive quitting and relaunching PieSwitcher.
- Both shortcut slots survive a system restart.
- The "back-compat / side-agnostic" flag on each migrated shortcut persists too — re-recording a shortcut in the new picker upgrades it to side-specific and clears the flag.

EDGE CASES
- The picker rejects shortcuts that would conflict with the system Escape / power button (does not capture Escape as a value).
- Capturing the same shortcut into both slots is allowed but pointless; an optional duplicate-warning hint is acceptable but not required.
- If the user holds a modifier while opening the Preferences window, the picker does NOT auto-capture it — capture mode only starts after the user clicks a slot.
- If the user records a modifier+key combination where the key is itself a modifier (e.g., Option + Shift), the picker treats it as a modifier-only shortcut (two modifiers held together), not as "Option + the Shift key".
- An unplugged or remapped keyboard does not break runtime matching — the matching is based on the key code identity, not the keyboard layout.

UNIT TESTS
- Tests cover recording a bare modifier (left vs right captured distinctly).
- Tests cover recording a modifier+key combination with the correct side preserved.
- Tests cover runtime matching: Right Option configured → Right Option fires, Left Option does NOT, Right Option + any other key does NOT.
- Tests cover both shortcut slots being active simultaneously and independently.
- Tests cover the migration from the checkbox UI (single modifier, multiple modifiers, zero modifiers).
- Tests cover the back-compat side-agnostic matching for migrated shortcuts vs side-specific matching for newly-recorded ones.
- Tests cover persistence (write → read back) for each slot and the side-agnostic flag.
- Tests cover Escape cancelling a capture in progress.
- Tests cover clearing Shortcut 2 returning the UI to the "Add second shortcut" state.

DOCS / DISCOVERABILITY
- A short helper line in Preferences explains that the picker accepts a single modifier held alone (e.g., Right Option) OR a modifier+key combination, and that left and right modifiers are distinct.
- A first-run / migration notice (one-time) tells upgrading users their previous configuration was preserved and points them to the new picker if they want to switch to side-specific matching.

title: Run scripts/bootstrap-release-secrets.sh to upload Apple Developer + notary secrets to GitHub
description:
Pre-flight blocker for Bringr-8pl.1.

Current state on origin (mekedron/PieSwitcher) — verified 2026-06-01 with `gh secret list`:

    SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY     2026-05-30T00:59:48Z

That is the ONLY GitHub Actions secret currently set. Every other secret the release workflow references is missing:

    MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64
    MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_PASSWORD
    MACOS_KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD
    MACOS_NOTARY_KEY_ID
    MACOS_NOTARY_ISSUER_ID
    MACOS_NOTARY_KEY_P8_BASE64
    HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN          (optional — bootstrap supports --skip-homebrew)

Why this blocks Bringr-8pl.1:
.github/workflows/release.yml gates signing + notarization on

    HAS_SIGNING: ${{ secrets.MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64 != '' && secrets.MACOS_NOTARY_KEY_P8_BASE64 != '' }}

With HAS_SIGNING=false the workflow falls back to `codesign --force --deep --sign -` (ad-hoc) and SKIPS the Notarize and DMG-Notarize steps entirely. The resulting .app would be Gatekeeper-blocked on a clean macOS user account, directly violating Bringr-8pl.1's acceptance criteria:
  - "No step is skipped due to a missing secret."
  - "The downloaded .app launches on a clean macOS user account (no Gatekeeper block...)"

So pushing a v*.*.* tag right now would publish a broken first release. The fix is upstream of pushing the tag: run the bootstrap script.

What to do:
1. Follow docs/release-setup.md from a fresh Apple Developer account to obtain:
   - A Developer ID Application .p12 (with the export passphrase)
   - An App Store Connect API key: .p8 file + issuer ID + key ID
   - (Optional) A GitHub PAT scoped to the Homebrew tap repo, or plan to use --skip-homebrew
2. From the repo root, run:
       ./scripts/bootstrap-release-secrets.sh
   or, if no Homebrew tap yet:
       ./scripts/bootstrap-release-secrets.sh --skip-homebrew
3. The script is interactive: it prompts for the .p12 path + passphrase (masked), the .p8 path, the issuer/key IDs, and the optional PAT. It auto-generates the random keychain password and re-uses the existing Sparkle key in the repo root unchanged.
4. Verify with:
       gh secret list
   Expected: all of MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64, MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_PASSWORD, MACOS_KEYCHAIN_PASSWORD, MACOS_NOTARY_KEY_ID, MACOS_NOTARY_ISSUER_ID, MACOS_NOTARY_KEY_P8_BASE64, SPARKLE_PRIVATE_KEY appear. HOMEBREW_TAP_TOKEN is optional.

After this is done, unblock Bringr-8pl.1 and proceed with the tag-and-push validation.

Scope: this bead is JUST the bootstrap-run step. It does not include touching the workflow YAML or the bootstrap script itself (those are Bringr-zkc, closed). If something goes wrong inside the bootstrap script, file follow-ups against Bringr-zkc.
notes:
2026-06-01 — Cannot be completed by an autonomous agent. The bootstrap script (scripts/bootstrap-release-secrets.sh) is interactive by design:

  - Prompts for filesystem paths to a Developer ID .p12 file and an App Store Connect .p8 key (artifacts only on the human's machine)
  - Masked passphrase prompt via stty -echo for the .p12 export passphrase
  - Prompts for App Store Connect issuer ID and key ID (sensitive identifiers the human holds)

Environment audit on the agent host: ~/Downloads, ~/Desktop, and the repo root contain no .p12 or .p8 files; no env vars hint at staged credentials. Keychain has 'Developer ID Application: Mikita Rabykin (A4HHRH8Y9M)' but exporting it to .p12 + supplying its passphrase is a human-presence action.

Pushing a v*.*.* tag would still ship a broken release without these secrets (per the original blocker analysis), so the only resolution path is: the human runs `./scripts/bootstrap-release-secrets.sh` (or `--skip-homebrew`) themselves, then re-opens this bead and Bringr-8pl.1.

Separately observed (out of scope): Bringr-8pl.1 is currently CLOSED in bd (commit f49f969) despite no tag being pushed and no GitHub Actions release run existing. That status appears wrong relative to its acceptance criteria, but fixing it is not in this bead's scope.

title: Publish the first PieSwitcher release and validate the full GitHub Actions workflow end-to-end
description:
Once the GitHub Actions workflows are in place and the GitHub secrets are populated (Bringr-zkc), the next step is to actually exercise the release pipeline by cutting and pushing the first version tag. This is the test that proves the bootstrap script set every secret correctly, the workflow YAML was ported correctly, and the resulting artifacts are downloadable, runnable, and update-checkable.

The goal here is NOT to ship feature work — it is to prove the release infrastructure works against a real tag, on the real GitHub Actions runners, against the real GitHub Releases page. Any failure in the run becomes a follow-up bead, fixed, and re-attempted; success means the team can ship every subsequent version by simply pushing a new tag.

The version number used for this first release is at the user's discretion (e.g., v0.0.1 or v0.1.0). What matters is that it is the first real run of the release workflow on the main branch.
acceptance_criteria:
PRE-FLIGHT
- The secrets-bootstrap script from Bringr-zkc has been run successfully, and the GitHub repo Settings → Secrets page shows every secret the release workflow references.
- The local working tree is clean and the main branch is up to date with origin.

TAG AND PUSH
- A version tag matching the v*.*.* pattern is created on the head of main and pushed to origin.
- Pushing the tag triggers the release workflow on GitHub Actions (visible in the Actions tab within ~1 minute of the push).

WORKFLOW RUN — END-TO-END SUCCESS
- The release workflow run finishes with status "success" (green).
- No step is skipped due to a missing secret. (If the Homebrew step was deliberately skipped at bootstrap, the run gates that step on the missing secret rather than failing.)
- The total runtime is reasonable (no obviously stuck step).

ARTIFACTS PUBLISHED
- A GitHub Release for the pushed tag appears at github.com/mekedron/Bringr/releases, with auto-generated release notes grouping recent conventional-commit subjects.
- The Release has at least one attached .app archive and one .dmg, both downloadable.
